The Court went through the list of the pending petitions, stated that it did not receive courtesy copies of James Kennedy's second report and account, that Mr. Skootsky and Mr. White need to provide a courtesy copy forthwith and that the petition has not been reviewed. The Court also stated that it received the joint case management statement, thanked the parties and asked if there are any further developments. Mr. Skootsky stated that mediation is ongoing, that settlement proposals have been exchanged most recently yesterday, but he had no chance to review it with his Client and that the parties agree Bette Epstein will continue to work with them. Mr. Skootsky also stated that the parties are serious about settlement, that he is optimistic that they will get something accomplished, that there has been very little discovery to date, and raised the issue of consolidating the petitions that are not set for trial and continuing the trial date. The Court stated that the oldest petition is three years old and informed Counsel that probate petitions will not linger in the department any longer. Mr. Thirkell stated that he is agreeable to continuing the trial date. The Court noted that it's been three years and discovery hasn't even begun, and stated that it is a waste of the court's resources. Mr. Skootsky stated that a lot of information has been provided outside of formal discovery. that Robert has served a lot of written discovery and that the deadline to respond is February 16th. Ms. Meisenheimer stated that there is a real efficiency in having all of these petitions heard at the same time and continuing the trial date continued to a date in July. The Court informed Counsel that the trial will go out to Dept. 206 and asked if they have thought about a Referee. Mr. Skootsky stated that the issue has not come up and that he does think the case needs a Referee to deal with the accountings. The Court noted that a Referee could assist with all of the issues. Mr. Thirkell stated that there is an issue of costs and that his Client has limited resources. Ms. Meisenheimer stated that she is not opposed, but she does not feel that a Referee is needed. The Court raised the issue of notice to the contingent beneficiaries. Mr. Skootsky stated that it is a pretty straight forward situation relative to notice, that both of the trusts terminated as of the death of Ms. Rosella and there were provisions regarding holding of two properties for twenty-five years, and that this is not a case where they have ongoing trusts. Mr. Thirkell stated that he would like to see the 2020 accounting at some point. Mr. Skootsky stated that the 2020 accounting has been prepared. Ms. Meisenheimer stated that the main concern is the ambiguity as to when the trial will occur. The Court stated that the parties will need to file a motion to continue the trial date and that is should be a modest continuance to a date when everyone will be ready for trial. Demurrer To Robert Kennedy?S Petition To Remove Trustee And For Redress Of Trustees Mismanagement And Misappropriation Of Trust Assets, As Amended
Off Calendar Sep-10-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Seth Skootsky appearing by Zoom for James Kennedy III (Trustee/Respondent/Moving Party). Robert White appearing by Zoom as Co-Counsel for James Kennedy III (Trustee/Respondent/Moving Party).
John J. Stein appearing via CourtCall for Robert Kennedy.
Edward Thirkell appearing by Zoom for William Kennedy.

Matter called and argument is held. Mr. Skootsky and Mr. Stein provided argument.

At the conclusion of argument, the court adopted the tentative ruling as follows: Before the court is Respondent and Trustee James J. Kennedy III's demurrers to the Second, Third, and Fourth Causes of Action of Petitioner and Beneficiary Robert Kennedy's Amended Petition to Remove Trustee and for Redress of Trustee's Mismanagement and Misappropriation of Trust Assets. The demurrer is OVERRULED.
The issue is whether assuming true the facts properly pled and facts properly noticed, do the challenged claims necessarily fail. (See Code Civ. Proc., section 430.10(e); Blank v. Kirwan (1985) 39 Cal.3d 311, 318.) "[I]f, on consideration of all facts stated, it appears that plaintiff is entitled to any relief against defendant, complaint will be held good, though facts may not be clearly stated, or may be intermingled with statement of other facts irrelevant to cause of action shown, or though plaintiff may demand relief to which he is not entitled under facts alleged." (Augustine v. Trucco (1954) 124 Cal.App.2d 229, 236, quoting Matteson v. Wagoner (1905) 147 Cal. 739, 742.)
Here, the challenged causes of action easily survive the demurrers. Petitioner alleges that Respondent, as Trustee, has breached his fiduciary duties to the beneficiaries, by using Trust property for his personal benefit and the benefit of his separate business; not charging his separate business market rates for the use of Trust assets; co-mingling his personal or business funds with Trust funds; using Trust funds for his own benefit and making disbarments of Trust funds to non-beneficiaries, and failing to account to the beneficiaries. The Petition alleges Respondent has breached his fiduciary duty by mismanaging or misappropriating Trust assets that were to flow (at least in part) to Petitioner as a beneficiary. On the face of the Petition, admitting the truth of all material facts properly pleaded, it appears that, if the allegations are true, Petitioner may be entitled to some kind of relief against Respondent, This is a sufficient showing to overcome Respondent's demurrer to the Second, Third, and Fourth causes of action on the basis of failure to state a cause of action.
The prayer and the demurred causes of action do seek various forms of monetary damages, including punitive damages, but even if the relief prayed for exceeds what is warranted by the pleaded facts-and the court is making no such finding, that is not grounds for demurrer. (Garris v. Mitchell (1935) 7 Cal.App.2d 430, 436.)

The time to respond is 10 days from the date of entry of the order. Mr. Stein is to prepare the form of order.

Judge: Joseph M. Quinn. Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda. Deputy: K. Kabasares. Matter not reported.
